title: Azure Logic Apps - Connection Object Structure And Managed API Reference
description: How connections work in Logic Apps: $connections parameter structure, connectionId format, managed API paths, authentication types, and how to reference connections in actions.

connections_overview.description: Connections are Azure resources that store credentials and endpoint information for managed connectors. In a Logic App workflow, connections are referenced by a key name in the $connections parameter. The actual connection resource (with credentials) lives in Azure as a Microsoft.Web/connections resource.

two_layer_design.layer_1_definition: Inside definition.parameters: declares $connections as an Object parameter with defaultValue {}. This makes $connections available as a runtime parameter inside the workflow.
two_layer_design.layer_2_values: Inside the outer parameters object (sibling to definition): provides the actual connection values at runtime. This is where the connectionId, id, and connectionName are specified.

connection_object_structure.description: Each entry in $connections.value is keyed by the connection reference name used in workflow expressions.

{connectionKeyName}.id: The managed API resource ID — points to the managed API definition in Azure
{connectionKeyName}.connectionId: The connection instance resource ID — points to the actual Microsoft.Web/connections resource
{connectionKeyName}.connectionName: The display name of the connection resource
{connectionKeyName}.connectionProperties: Optional. Contains authentication settings like ManagedServiceIdentity.

notes: The connectionKeyName is arbitrary — you choose it when adding the connection in the designer. It must match exactly what you use in @parameters('$connections')['keyName'] expressions.
The id field references the managed API template (same for everyone in the same region)
The connectionId references the specific connection resource in your subscription (unique per connection)

managed_api_name_reference.description: The apiName segment in the managed API 'id' path. This is the canonical name for each connector's managed API.

azuresentinel.notes: Most common. Used in both trigger and action connections.

Azuresentinel.notes: Capital A variant seen in MSI-authenticated connections (microsoftsentinel key)

azuread.display_name: Azure Active Directory / Microsoft Entra ID

wdatp.display_name: Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (Windows Defender ATP)

service_account_oauth.description: A user account (service account) authorized the connection via OAuth. The connection stores a refresh token.
notes: The connection was authorized by a user clicking 'Authorize' in the portal
If the service account password changes, the connection breaks and must be re-authorized

managed_service_identity.description: The Logic App's system-assigned managed identity is used to authenticate the connection. No stored credentials.
prerequisites: Logic App must have system-assigned managed identity enabled (identity.type = SystemAssigned)
The managed identity must have the required role on the Sentinel workspace (Microsoft Sentinel Responder or similar)
The connection resource must be created with MSI authentication type
benefits: No credentials to rotate or expire
Works well in CI/CD and automated deployments
Audit trail shows the Logic App's managed identity, not a user account

action.pattern: Same as trigger — host.connection.name points to the connectionId via $connections

multiple_connections_same_api_patterns.description: It is common to use multiple connections to the same managed API in one workflow. Each gets a unique key name.

trigger_vs_action.description: Trigger uses one Sentinel connection (often MSI for webhook registration), actions use another (service account for API calls)

example.azuresentinel: Trigger connection (MSI or service account for /incident-creation webhook)
example.azuresentinel_1: Action connection (used for Entities API, Watchlist API calls)

suffix_numbering.description: When a second connection to the same API is needed, suffix _1, _2 etc. are appended to the key name

descriptive_naming.description: Some playbooks use descriptive names instead of numeric suffixes
examples: azuresentinel-Send-to-SharePoint for MSI connection in SharePoint playbook

arm_template_connection_parameters.description: When deploying via ARM template, connections are injected as parameters. The $connections value is provided as a parameter to the Logic App resource.
notes: In parameterized ARM deployments, use resourceId() and subscription() functions instead of hardcoded GUIDs
The Microsoft.Web/connections resource must be deployed before the Logic App resource (use dependsOn)
Connection resources require their own ARM resource blocks with the API type and auth parameters
